goal_10.config.title: Combining Income Growth with Inequality Reduction Benefits Everyone
goal_10.config.abstract: Most inequality measures fail to account for overall economic development. The Prosperity Gap tracks both economic progress and its distribution. Although prosperity has improved globally, many economies—particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa—have regressed in recent decades.
goal_10.config.citation: Lønborg, J H, Lambrechts, M & Yonzan, N. 2026. “Combining income growth with inequality reduction benefits everyone” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by D Mahler, A F Pirlea, U Serajuddin, A Thudt, D Wadhwa, and M Welch. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO. https://www.doi.org/10.60616/pp3f-d004[emphasis: https://doi.org/10.60616/f7gk-me57]
goal_10.config.inputs: Aart Kraay, Miki Khanh Doan, Maria Eugenia Genoni, Christoph Lakner
goal_10.config.homeFactoidText: individuals worldwide live below the global prosperity standard of $28 per day
goal_10.config.homeTagline: The Prosperity Gap in Sub-Saharan Africa is at the same level as the world was in the 1980s, making the region roughly four decades behind the global average.
goal_10.content.#1.text: It is common to measure economic development by examining a country’s growth in gross domestic product (GDP) per capita. But this metric says nothing about how progress is shared among the wider population. Although it is important that the income of the poor continue to increase, it is better if their income grows as quickly—or faster than—the income of the rich. When this happens, prosperity is shared more fairly and inequality decreases. High levels of inequality impedes poverty reduction and slows shared progress.[reference: Bourguignon2003][reference: Bergstrom2022][reference: Lakner2022] Inequalities today also signal differences in opportunities in early life usually stemming from factors that cannot be changed, such as gender, race, family background, or birthplace.[reference: Brunori2013] [reference: Chetty2017] [reference: Corak2013]
goal_10.content.#2.text: To that end, in line with SGD 10 to reduce inequality within and across countries, the World Bank has started tracking the[emphasis:  number of economies with high inequality], defined as economies with a Gini index over 40.[reference: Haddad2024] You can find a visual explanation of how the Gini index works in the 2023 Atlas.[reference: MahlerBaur2023] In 2024, inequality was high in 43 of the 169 economies with data to calculate the Gini index. Compared to 72 economies with high inequality in 1995, this progress has been substantial.
goal_10.content.#3.title: The number of economies with high inequality has almost halved in the past three decades
goal_10.content.#3.subtitle: Economies with high inequality have a Gini index greater than 40; those with low inequality have a Gini index less than 30
goal_10.content.#3.source: Key Inequality Indicators, Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026).
goal_10.content.#3.visdescription: The figure reports the number of economies with a Gini index over 40 for 1995–2024. The number of economies with high inequality has fallen from 72 in 1995 to 43 in 2024. The number of economies with low inequality, defined as those with Gini index under 30, has increased from 22 in 1995 to 33 in 2024.
goal_10.content.#3.keyFactLabel: economies have high inequality today, compared to 72 in 1995.
goal_10.content.#3.scenes.#0.text: The number of economies with high inequality (Gini index > 40) has decreased substantially since the 1990s. In 1995, 72 economies had high inequality; by 2024, this had fallen to 43—nearly half the number in 1995.
goal_10.content.#3.scenes.#1.text: At the same time, the number of economies with low inequality (Gini index < 30) increased from 22 in 1995 to 33 in 2024, underlining the progress in reducing inequality.
goal_10.content.#4.text: The map below uses the latest household survey to report the Gini index for each economy. Looking at the regional pattern, we see that high inequality is concentrated in Latin America & the Caribbean and Sub-Saharan Africa. High inequality in Latin American economies is well known[reference: Alvaredo2023], whereas similar disparities in Sub-Saharan African economies are only beginning to receive attention.[reference: David2025]
goal_10.content.#5.text: Sub-Saharan Africa[emphasis:  faces high levels of both poverty and inequality].[emphasis:  ]Despite this, [emphasis: Benin ]is among the economies that have managed to move from high to moderate inequality.[reference: WorldBank2025] Here, a combination of high economic growth and a stronger tax collection system has increased domestic resource mobilization, enabling an expansion of social safety net programs targeting the poorest.
goal_10.content.#6.title: Economies with high inequality are concentrated in Latin America and Sub-Saharan Africa
goal_10.content.#6.subtitle: The map shows the Gini index of each economy using the most recent household survey data
goal_10.content.#6.source: Key Inequality Indicators, Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026).
goal_10.content.#6.visdescription: The map shows the levels of inequality across the world.  Today, economies with high inequality (Gini index > 40) are concentrated in Latin America & the Caribbean and Sub-Saharan Africa.
goal_10.content.#7.text: Although it is clear that the number of economies with high inequality has decreased over recent decades, this metric says little about how much the economies grew or which groups experienced progress. In extreme cases, an improvement in the Gini index could be due to the poorest experiencing proportionally smaller income losses than the wealthy, as happened in some cases during the COVID-19 pandemic. In such a case, a reduction in the Gini index would not be the result of progress towards shared prosperity.
goal_10.content.#9.text: Now, let’s take a look at a measure that focuses on  the bottom end of the income distribution.
goal_10.content.#10.text: Comparing the growth experienced by the poorest 40 percent of the population to the average income growth of the entire population is one way to assess progress of the poor.[reference: WorldBank2025shared] Progress is more equitable when income growth for the poorest 40 percent of the population is faster than the average in the economy. In many cases, an economy’s outcome using this method aligns quite well with the changes in the Gini index.
goal_10.content.#11.text: So, let’s see how economies perform when we compare the growth of the poorest 40 percent with the average income growth of the entire population.
goal_10.content.#12.title: In three out of four economies, income growth of the poorest 40 percent was faster than the population’s average between 2016 and 2021
goal_10.content.#12.subtitle: Progress is equitable when the income of the poorest 40 percent grows faster than the average income
goal_10.content.#12.fasterlabel: Faster growth for the poorest 40 percent
goal_10.content.#12.slowerlabel: Slower growth for the poorest 40 percent
goal_10.content.#12.xaxistitle: Income growth of the poorest 40 percent (%)
goal_10.content.#12.visdescription: The figure compares the growth rates of average national income and average income of the poorest 40 percent of the population. In three out of four economies, income growth  for the poorest 40 percent was better than the average income.
goal_10.content.#12.keyFactLabel: economies experienced faster income growth of the poorest 40 percent compared to average income growth.
goal_10.content.#12.scenes.#0.text: Each dot in the chart corresponds to the income growth of a particular economy, colored by region. The 45-degree line, also known as the [emphasis: line of equality], reflects equal growth rates in the incomes of the poorest 40 percent and the entire population. Data points located to the right of this line indicate that income among the poorest 40 percent increased more rapidly than the national average income.
goal_10.content.#12.scenes.#1.text: Between 2016 and 2021, the income of the poorest 40 percent in [emphasis: Bolivia ]increased by more than four percent, whereas the average income grew by less than one percent. In contrast, the poorest 40 percent in [emphasis: Jamaica ]experienced a decline in their income while the average income rose by over two percent. Although both nations saw overall economic growth, income gains in [emphasis: Bolivia ]were more evenly distributed, benefiting lower-income groups, while [emphasis: Jamaica ]observed a relative decrease in prosperity among the poorer segments of its population.
goal_10.content.#12.scenes.#2.text: However, although measuring income growth within the poorest 40 percent offers a practical means to assess shared prosperity, it does not capture all aspects of inequality and presents certain limitations. For example, although we can compare income growth of the poorest 40 percent across Latin American & the Caribbean economies, it is hard to see which economy has improved the most given their starting positions.
goal_10.content.#12.scenes.#3.text: For example, income growth among Brazil’s poorest 40 percent is lower than Mexico’s, but the average Brazilian is still richer than the average Mexican. The growth of the poorest 40 percent measure does not capture this difference.
goal_10.content.#13.text: Calculating growth of the poorest 40 percent also requires comparable surveys across two years. As a result, the chart only reports growth for 88 economies using data from years around 2016 and 2021. There are not enough recent comparable surveys to calculate growth for a meaningful number of economies beyond 2021.
goal_10.content.#14.text: A thorough evaluation of prosperity requires analyzing income levels, growth rates, and how gains are shared among the population. This is crucial for identifying vulnerable groups and designing targeted interventions. And if such interventions are to be impactful, analysis needs to be timely in order to be accurate and relevant. The measure introduced below attempts to do this.
goal_10.content.#15.text: The Prosperity Gap: combining inequality and progress of the poor
goal_10.content.#16.text: The World Bank has introduced the [emphasis: Global Prosperity Gap] as a key vision indicator [reference: WBvision] for monitoring inclusive progress. This measure integrates both average income growth and advancements in equity—reflecting reductions in inequality—into a single metric, assigning the greatest emphasis to growth among the lowest-income segments of the population.
goal_10.content.#17.text: The [emphasis: Prosperity Gap] is [emphasis: the average factor by which individuals’ incomes must be multiplied to reach a global prosperity standard]. This standard is currently set at $28 per person per day, which is approximately equal to the average income of a person living in an economy that is at the threshold of high-income status. [reference: kraay2023]
goal_10.content.#18.title: Global prosperity standard
goal_10.content.#18.label: Approximately equivalent to the average income of a typical individual living in an economy that is graduating from middle- to high-income status.
goal_10.content.#19.text: The Prosperity Gap is a measure that gives higher emphasis to the level of income of the poor. Consider an individual earning $14 a day and another earning $7 a day. To reach the $28 a day standard, the first individual must [emphasis: double ]their income while the second must increase their income [emphasis: fourfold]. Consequently, the Prosperity Gap is two for the first individual and four for the second. In other words, this measure gives the person with half the income double the emphasis.
goal_10.content.#20.text: We can calculate the Prosperity Gap for individuals or for any defined population group. So, the Prosperity Gap of the population can be separated to reflect the contribution of each individual. This approach lets us analyze data in more detail and tailor policy suggestions for sub-population groups, unlike the Gini index or poorest 40 percent income growth measure, which only look at groups as a whole.
goal_10.content.#21.text: To understand how the measure works, let’s consider two economies: Colombia and Peru.
goal_10.content.#22.title: The Prosperity Gap tracks both income level and its distribution
goal_10.content.#22.subtitle: Prosperity Gaps for all individuals  in Peru and Colombia, grouped into percentiles
goal_10.content.#22.source: Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026).
goal_10.content.#22.visdescription: This chart shows the Prosperity Gap for each percentile of the income distributions in Peru and Colombia.
goal_10.content.#22.scenes.#0.text: Suppose that there are 100 people in each economy ranked from poorest on the left to richest on the right. The height of each bar represents the income of each individual in 2026.
goal_10.content.#22.scenes.#1.text: The Prosperity Gap measures the income multiple necessary to reach the global prosperity standard of $28 per day. The smaller the level of income, the larger the increase necessary to close the gap.
goal_10.content.#22.scenes.#2.text: For example, the income of the 10th poorest person in Peru is close to $4 and in Colombia, it is close to $3.50.
goal_10.content.#22.scenes.#3.text: The Peruvian with a $4 income needs to increase their income [emphasis: seven times] to reach the prosperity standard, whereas the Colombian with a $3.50 income needs to increase their income [emphasis: eight times].
goal_10.content.#22.scenes.#4.text: Each brick represents the person's level of income. The number of bricks represents how many times their income needs to increase. Individuals with lower incomes require a substantially greater increase in income to reach the prosperity standard.
goal_10.content.#22.scenes.#5.text: For example, the [emphasis: 10th poorest ]Colombian would need to increase their income [emphasis: eight times] while the [emphasis: 20th poorest] would need to increase it just over [emphasis: five times] (both are highlighted in the chart).
goal_10.content.#22.scenes.#6.text: It is important to note that individuals with lower income in Colombia face greater challenges in achieving the prosperity standard compared to similar individuals in Peru. The Prosperity Gaps of the poorest 10 people are much higher in Colombia than in Peru.
goal_10.content.#22.scenes.#7.text: A nation’s Prosperity Gap is the average gap of all individuals in that nation. The Prosperity Gap is higher in Colombia (4.5) than in Peru (3.5), reflecting the larger gaps among the poor in Colombia.
goal_10.content.#23.text: If we only consider the Gini index, we could see that Colombia (with 54) is more unequal than Peru (with 41) today. If we instead only looked at income levels, people in Colombia earn more on average ($20 per day) than people in Peru ($14 per day).   [emphasis: The Prosperity Gap rewards higher levels of income while also imposing a “penalty” for inequality.  ]In the case of Colombia and Peru, the negative effect of higher inequality in Colombia outweighs its higher average income – the Prosperity Gap is larger in Columbia than in Peru.[emphasis: ]
goal_10.content.#24.text: Next, we’ll look at the Prosperity Gap of economies, regions, and the world in 2026, and consider trends to highlight changes over time. Then we will break down these trends to examine the roles that mean income and inequality play in shaping the Prosperity Gap.
goal_10.content.#25.text: Linking the national Prosperity Gaps to regional and global Prosperity Gaps
goal_10.content.#26.text: Just like an economy’s Prosperity Gap is simply the average Prosperity Gap of everyone in the economy, the region’s or world’s Prosperity Gap is the average across everyone in the region or the world. This is important, as the global gap in prosperity can easily connect to regional, national, or individual gaps.
goal_10.content.#27.title: From national gaps to regional and global gaps
goal_10.content.#27.subtitle: A group’s Prosperity Gap is the average gap of all its members
goal_10.content.#27.source: Authors’ calculation using Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026).
goal_10.content.#27.visdescription: This chart shows national Prosperity Gaps aggregated to regional and global Prosperity Gaps.
goal_10.content.#27.scenes.#0.text: In 2026, the Prosperity Gap for Colombia was 4.5 and for Peru, 3.5.
goal_10.content.#27.scenes.#1.text: Each square represents an economy’s Prosperity Gap in 2026, colored by region and reflecting the population size.
goal_10.content.#27.scenes.#2.text: There is a large difference in prosperity between economies. In some, such as the United States, Japan, and Estonia, incomes are almost high enough to meet the prosperity standard, but in others, such as South Sudan and Mozambique, incomes would have to grow roughly 25 times to reach that level.
goal_10.content.#27.scenes.#3.text: The regional Prosperity Gaps reflect the average gap of all individuals living in the region. This is equivalent to taking the average Prosperity Gap across economies while accounting for the size of their population.
goal_10.content.#27.scenes.#4.text: Today, most people in Europe & Central Asia and North America have reached the prosperity standard of $28 per day. On average, people in Latin America & the Caribbean need to increase their incomes about [emphasis: three times] to reach that same level of prosperity. In South Asia and the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan, incomes need to be about [emphasis: five times] higher, and in Sub-Saharan Africa, almost [emphasis: 11 times] higher.
goal_10.content.#27.scenes.#5.text: The Global Prosperity Gap combines the Prosperity Gaps of every person in the world. Today, individual incomes worldwide would need to rise nearly [emphasis: five times] on average for everyone to reach the prosperity standard of $28 per day.
goal_10.content.#28.text: Nations can learn from the East Asian case, where incomes grew rapidly in the past few decades, helping them reduce the Prosperity Gap considerably from close to 21 in 1981 to 2.5 today. In the next section, we will take a closer look at these trends.
goal_10.content.#29.title: Global Prosperity Gap
goal_10.content.#29.label: Incomes today need to increase nearly [emphasis: five times, ]on average, for everyone to reach the prosperity standard of $28 a day.
goal_10.content.#30.text: Tracking the Prosperity Gap over time
goal_10.content.#31.text: Looking at the Prosperity Gap over time can be useful to understand the patterns of development. A reduction in the Prosperity Gap signals an improvement of welfare.
goal_10.content.#32.title: Significant global progress hides regional variations
goal_10.content.#32.subtitle: While the global Prosperity Gap has decreased over the last four decades, regional and national differences are substantial
goal_10.content.#32.source: Authors’ calculation using Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026).
goal_10.content.#32.visdescription: This chart reports the trend in Prosperity Gap from 1981 to 2026. It reports the trends for the world, regions, and select countries.
goal_10.content.#32.keyFactLabel: behind the rest of the world, Sub-Saharan Africa continues to face a significant gap in prosperity.
goal_10.content.#32.scenes.#0.text: The Global Prosperity Gap has more than halved from over [emphasis: 13 ]in [emphasis: 1981 ]to  less than [emphasis: five ]in [emphasis: 2026]. This substantial progress reflects the poorer nations and people catching up to the richer nations and people.
goal_10.content.#32.scenes.#1.text: All regions have made significant progress since 1981, with East Asia and Pacific standing out, having the largest (21) to the third lowest gap (2.5).
goal_10.content.#32.scenes.#2.text: But the most recent decade has seen stagnation in the Prosperity Gaps in two regions: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan and Sub-Saharan Africa, with the latter’s Prosperity Gap today close to the global gap in the 1980s. By this metric, the region is around 40 years behind global progress.
goal_10.content.#32.scenes.#3.text: Colombia’s and Peru’s Prosperity Gaps have experienced similar developments in recent decades, including periods of increasing gaps, most notably during the COVID-19 pandemic.
goal_10.content.#32.scenes.#4.text: In Ethiopia, the level today is half what it was in the 1980s, although this hides faster progress in the 1990s and early 2000s, and a stagnation in recent decades.
goal_10.content.#32.scenes.#5.text: Worryingly, in some Sub-Saharan African economies, such as Mozambique, improvements in the Prosperity Gap have stalled over the last two decades, and recently worsened.
goal_10.content.#33.text: The Prosperity Gap improves when average income increases, inequality decreases, or both
goal_10.content.#34.text: Changes in the Prosperity Gap can be due to [emphasis: changes in average income, inequality, ]or[emphasis:  both]. To illustrate this, let’s consider scenarios where only average income changes, to a scenario where both inequality and income changes.
goal_10.content.#35.text: Consider a scenario where everyone earns 10 percent higher income. This raises everyone’s income equally in relative terms, hence, relative inequality stays unchanged. For example, say person A had an initial income of $10 and person B, $20. With a 10 percent increase, A earns $11 and B earns $22. The initial ratio of income between A and B ($20/$10 = 2) remains the same even after the increase ($22/$11 = 2). Since inequality is unchanged, all the change in the Prosperity Gap in that economy is due to the change in average income alone.
goal_10.content.#36.title: Raise everyone’s income by 10 percent
goal_10.content.#36.textParagraph: A 10 percent increase in everyone’s income will [emphasis: increase ]the average income by $2 from $20 to $22. As a result, the Prosperity Gap [emphasis: decreases] by 10 percent from 4.5 to 4.0.
goal_10.content.#36.source: Authors’ calculation, Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026).
goal_10.content.#36.reality: Initial level of income and Prosperity Gap
goal_10.content.#36.visdescription: This chart reports the changes in average income and Prosperity Gap for a scenario where everyone’s income is raised by 10 percent.
goal_10.content.#37.text: Next, consider what happens to the Prosperity Gap if every person’s income increases by $10 instead of 10 percent. This raises everyone’s income, including the average national income, by $10. The difference here is that the $10 increase is a relatively larger increase for those with lower incomes and a relatively smaller increase for those with higher incomes.
goal_10.content.#38.text: Consider again the same example, with person A earning an initial income of $10 and person B, $20. With a $10 increase, A now earns $20 and B earns $30. This means that the initial ratio of income for A and B ($20/$10 = 2) has now been reduced ($30/$20 = 1.5). With the $10 increase in income, the difference in income between the two individuals has narrowed. In this case, any change in the Prosperity Gap of the economy is due to the change in both average income and inequality.
goal_10.content.#39.title: Raise everyone’s income by $10
goal_10.content.#39.textParagraph: If everyone earns an extra $10, the average income increases from $20 to $30, and the Prosperity Gap falls from 4.5 to just over 1. One-third of this change in the Prosperity Gap is driven by an increase in average income; the rest is driven by the reduction in inequality.
goal_10.content.#39.source: Authors’ calculation, Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026).
goal_10.content.#39.reality: Initial level of income and Prosperity Gap
goal_10.content.#39.visdescription: This chart reports the changes in average income and Prosperity Gap for a scenario where everyone’s income is raised by $10.
goal_10.content.#40.text: Find out what happens to the Prosperity Gap for any country
goal_10.content.#41.text: The graphic below shows how much a change in income can affect an economy’s Prosperity Gap. There are two ways to change income: (a) the dollar amount (absolute change), and (b) a percent amount (relative change). Selecting only the dollar amount (changing absolute income) will not change inequality, but selecting the percent amount (changing relative income) will change both inequality and average income. Type in the name of any economy and adjust the absolute and relative income sliders below to see changes in their Prosperity Gaps.
goal_10.content.#42.title: Choose an economy to see the changes in their Prosperity Gap
goal_10.content.#42.source: Authors’ calculation, Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026).
goal_10.content.#42.visdescription: This visualization reports the changes in the Prosperity Gap due to income increase, in percent or $, for an economy of choice.
goal_10.content.#44.text: The world has made a lot of progress in the last 40 years. The Prosperity Gap has fallen by more than half. A large part of this progress is due to people’s incomes in large populous economies in Asia catching up to their counterparts in richer economies.[reference: GarciaRojas2025] But there is still a long way to go. Worldwide, people need to increase their incomes by nearly five times, on average, to reach the prosperity standard of a typical person in an economy moving into high-income status.
goal_10.content.#45.text: Prosperity Gaps vary by region. In East Asia & Pacific, which had the highest Prosperity Gap (21) in 1981, incomes today need to grow close to three times, on average, to reach the global prosperity standard. In Sub-Saharan Africa, on the other hand, incomes must increase at least 11 times to reach the same standard, placing the region at the level equivalent to the global gap in the 1980s. Most of the global progress has come from high income growth in populous Asian economies, although reduced inequality has made a significant contribution.[reference: WorldBank2024]
goal_10.content.#46.text: To further close the prosperity gaps, high income growth is a prerequisite, not just for economies in Sub-Saharan, but also for those in South Asia and the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan. In some regions, particularly Sub-Saharan Africa and Latin America & the Caribbean, ongoing high levels of inequality require continued attention to improve prosperity.
goal_10.references.Alvaredo2023: Alvaredo, F, Bourguignon, F, Ferreira, F H G and Lustig, N. 2023. “Seventy-five Years of Measuring Income Inequality in Latin America.” IDB Publications [preprint].
goal_10.references.Bergstrom2022: Bergstrom, K. 2022. “The Role of Income Inequality for Poverty Reduction.” The World Bank Economic Review 36(3): 583–604.
goal_10.references.Bourguignon2003: Bourguignon, F. 2003. “The Growth Elasticity of Poverty Reduction: Explaining Heterogeneity across Countries and Time Periods.” In Inequality and Growth: Theory and Policy Implications, edited by T S Eicher and S J Turnovsky. Cambridge: MIT Press.
goal_10.references.Brunori2013: Brunori, P, Ferreira, F H G and Peragine, V. 2013. “Inequality of Opportunity, Income Inequality, and Economic Mobility: Some International Comparisons.” In Getting Development Right: Structural Transformation, Inclusion, and Sustainability in the Post-Crisis Era, edited by E Paus, 85–115. New York: Palgrave Macmillan.

goal_10.references.Corak2013: Corak, M. 2013. “Income Inequality, Equality of Opportunity, and Intergenerational Mobility.” Journal of Economic Perspectives 27(3): 79–102.

Forthcoming. “Global Inequality and Economic Growth: The Three Decades before Covid-19 and Three Decades After.” In: Oxford Handbook of Income Distribution and Economic Growth, edited by G Anderson and S Bandyopadhyay. Oxford University Press.
goal_10.references.Haddad2024: Haddad, C N ⓡ Mahler, D G ⓡ Diaz-Bonilla, C ⓡ Hill, R ⓡ Lakner, C ⓡ Lara Ibarra, G. 2024. “The World Bank’s New Inequality Indicator: The Number of Countries with High Inequality (English).” Policy Research Working Paper 10796, Washington DC: World Bank.
goal_10.references.kraay2023: Kraay, A ⓡ Lakner, C ⓡ Özler, B ⓡ Decerf, B ⓡ Jolliffe, D ⓡ Sterck, O ⓡ Yonzan, N. 2023. “A New Distribution Sensitive Index for Measuring Welfare, Poverty, and Inequality.” Policy Research Working Paper 10470. Washington DC: World Bank.
goal_10.references.Lakner2022: Lakner, C, Gerszon Mahler, D, Negre, M and Prydz, E B. 2022. “How Much Does Reducing Inequality Matter for Global Poverty?” The Journal of Economic Inequality 20(3): 559–85.
goal_10.references.MahlerBaur2023: Mahler, Daniel Gerszon, and Dominikus Baur. 2023. “Progress and setbacks in reducing income inequalities” In Atlas of Sustainable Development Goals 2023, edited by A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
goal_10.references.WorldBank2024: World Bank. 2024. “Poverty, Prosperity, and Planet Report 2024: Pathways Out of the Polycrisis.” Washington DC.: World Bank.
goal_10.references.WorldBank2025: World Bank. 2025. Perspectives économiques du Bénin: Accroître la mobilisation des recettes intérieures tout en protégeant les pauvres. Washington DC: World Bank.
goal_10.references.WorldBank2025shared: World Bank. 2015. A Measured Approach to Ending
